text: Artificial intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly present in all areas of our daily lives and while they can provide many benefits, they can also pose a significant threat to both individual and collective interests. For this reason, regulatory frameworks are being introduced at the international level in order to limit the undesirable consequences of AI. While most regulations are limited to ethics guidelines and non-binding recommendations, the European Union decided to introduce binding legislation on this matter. In Spring 2024, the Artificial Intelligence Act (the AI Act) was adopted, which entered into force on 1 August 2024 and whose main provisions will be implemented two years later. This regulation is unprecedented in that it aims to regulate artificial intelligence on a general basis for all sectors, rather than on a case by case basis, hence it can be qualified as the first artificial intelligence regulation. As a pioneer in terms of international AI regulation and in view of its extraterritorial nature, the Act is set to significantly influence the integration of AI technologies in Europe and possibly worldwide.
